evidence: Dependency direction is consistently inward: api/ imports from exploration/, model/, config/, query/; exploration/ imports from query/, model/, config/; query/ imports only model/; config/ imports model/ and utils/ — no upward imports visible.
LongIntHashMap lives in a dedicated collections/ package; it is referenced by query-layer code (CollocateQueryHelper) — a clean containment for a specialized data structure with no package cycle.

RelationUtils in api/ maps short aliases to grammar-config IDs; this is business-domain knowledge about relation naming that arguably belongs in config/ but is isolated in one small class, limiting blast radius.
SemanticFieldExplorer is public in exploration/ and aggregates MultiSeedExplorer (package-private) and CollocateProfileComparator (public) — the internal helper is properly scoped.

ExplorationHandlers in api/ creates a SemanticFieldExplorer directly in WordSketchApiServer's constructor, tying the api and exploration layers together at construction time rather than through an interface.
QueryExecutor interface in query/ provides the only formal boundary between the api/exploration layers and the BlackLab implementation — all callers use the interface, not the concrete class.
No circular dependencies detected: api → exploration → query → model is a strict DAG.

Top-level packages map cleanly to domain capabilities: api/ = HTTP layer, exploration/ = semantic field analysis, query/ = corpus querying, model/ = domain value objects, config/ = grammar configuration, indexer/ = corpus ingestion, utils/ = shared primitives, viz/ = rendering.

ExplorationResult is used as the shared return type for both single-seed (exploreByPattern) and multi-seed (exploreMultiSeed) exploration — the 'seed' field becomes a comma-joined string for multi-seed results, which is a semantic overloading of the single-seed model.

MultiSeedExplorer was correctly extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer; SemanticFieldExplorer is now a facade with exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, and fetchExamples — four distinct algorithms sharing one public class.
package-info.java for the model package documents naming conventions (JavaBeans vs record accessor style) — shows deliberate authorship.
ExploreOptions record is well-scoped and named; its Javadoc note about the 'Explore vs Exploration' naming asymmetry is an honest acknowledgment of a minor inconsistency.
CollocateProfileComparator has a @Deprecated no-arg-config constructor with no tracked removal timeline, leaving two construction paths alive without a clear migration owner.
The 'collections' and 'viz' packages each contain exactly one file (LongIntHashMap and RadialPlot respectively), making them thin wrappers around single classes rather than true domain groupings.

QueryExecutor interface has one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) but the abstraction is justified: it isolates the expensive BlackLab dependency from the exploration and api layers and enables testability. The interface contract is coherent and documents each method's distinct role.

ExplorationHandlers contains three private static methods — formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates — that are pure pass-throughs to ExploreResponseBuilder with identical signatures. They add no behavior, policy, or translation.
ExploreOptions record bundles four tuning parameters; it is used in exactly one call path (SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern). This is a focused, paid-for abstraction.
ValidatedExploreRequest private record in ExplorationHandlers is an appropriate internal DTO that eliminates a multi-return-value parse-and-validate pattern.
GrammarConfigLoader is a pure static-factory loader; callers receive GrammarConfig value objects — the abstraction boundary is clean and the loader has no stateful surface.
RelationConfig.buildFullPattern() encapsulates pattern substitution; callers do not manipulate pattern strings directly.
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer is a thin wrapper, but it adds genuine cross-cutting policy (IAE→400, IOException→500, catch-all→500) — it pays for its existence.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern has a wide parameter list now replaced by ExploreOptions, but exploreMultiSeed still takes four loose parameters instead of using ExploreOptions (the record's Javadoc even notes that nounsPerSeed does not apply to multi-seed, explaining the asymmetry).
One-impl interface count flagged in scan (1) maps to QueryExecutor — as argued above, this is justified.
Scan sub_axes: abstraction_leverage 100, interface_honesty 92, delegation_density 100, definition_directness 100, type_discipline 100 — mechanical analysis sees a clean picture.

pom.xml declares: blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser — both necessary for BlackLab corpus querying (distinct artifacts with distinct roles: engine = index access, query-parser = CQL/BCQL parsing).
fastjson2 — single JSON library used consistently throughout; no competing JSON library (e.g., Jackson or Gson) is present.
slf4j-api + logback-classic — standard, intentional pairing for SLF4J facade with Logback backend; no multiple logging frameworks.
jspecify — lightweight @Nullable/@NonNull annotation library; not a framework, minimal footprint.
junit-jupiter-api, junit-jupiter-engine, junit-platform-launcher — all test-scoped, consistent JUnit 5 setup.
No moment/dayjs, no axios/fetch, no lodash equivalents — the Java dependency surface is appropriately minimal for the domain.
No unused imports or pom entries visible; every listed dependency is actively referenced in production sources.
BlackLab brings Lucene as a transitive dependency; this is expected and unavoidable given the project's use of BlackLab as the index engine.

issues_preventing_higher_score: The com.sun.net.httpserver package (JDK internal HTTP server) is used as the HTTP framework without a declared dependency — this works but is an implicit JDK internal API that could break across JDK versions.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Use of com.sun.net.httpserver (JDK internal HTTP server API) is an undeclared implicit dependency; it works on current JDKs but is not a stable public API.

evidence: wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer uses three typed catch blocks (IAE→400, IOException→500, Exception→500) with distinct log levels (warn vs error) — direct and intention-revealing.

validateExploreRequest in ExplorationHandlers uses a guard-clause early-return pattern consistently: parse → validate seeds → validate relation → validate params → return DTO. Each failure path returns null and sends its own error.
LongIntHashMap implements open-addressing with linear probing cleanly: put, get, containsKey all follow the same probe loop; rehash rebuilds without touching the logic path.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses a depth counter to handle nested brackets; the logic correctly skips quoted strings both inside and outside brackets, and handles backslash escapes.

buildAdjectiveProfileList in CollocateProfileComparator creates three separate double[] arrays (scores, then streams for avg, max, min) over the same nounScores.values() data, where a single pass would suffice.

logTopProfiles in CollocateProfileComparator contains complex lambda chains for debug logging; the method is only called at DEBUG level but the stream setup cost is paid unconditionally (no logger.isDebugEnabled() guard).

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial wraps its entire body in a try-catch(Exception) and calls sendError — even though it is already wrapped by wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer. The inner catch silently overrides the outer handler's exception classification.

handleBcqlQueryPost in SketchHandlers has its own four-clause try-catch block (IOException, JSONException, IllegalArgumentException, Exception) that duplicates the exception taxonomy of wrapHandler, producing redundant error handling on the same call path.
The ExploreParams private record in ExplorationHandlers is a well-scoped internal value that reduces the argument count from 4 to 1 without leaking beyond the class.

ExplorationHandlers → ExploreResponseBuilder boundary is clean: handlers call populateExploreResponse and formatAdjectiveProfile; all model-to-JSON translation is in ExploreResponseBuilder with no leakage into the handler.
ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer boundary passes ExploreOptions and RelationConfig value objects — minimal, typed inputs, no raw strings passed across the seam.
ValidatedExploreRequest record captures validated parse state before the domain call — a clean hand-off object that separates HTTP parsing from domain invocation.
CORS handling is fully centralised in HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader, called by every response-sending method — consistent lifecycle at the HTTP boundary.

handleSemanticFieldComparison does not use validateExploreRequest (which is correct — it has different required params), but it duplicates the param-parsing preamble (parseBaseExploreRequest + requireParam + resolveExploreParams). The two paths are not unified at the seam.
ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges is overloaded for ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult — appropriate polymorphism, both overloads have the same conceptual contract.

ExplorationResult is used as the wire type between MultiSeedExplorer and ExplorationHandlers; the 'seed' string in multi-seed results is a joined CSV, and DiscoveredNoun objects represent seeds, not discovered nouns. This semantic mismatch at the seam forces ExplorationHandlers to interpret the result differently for single vs multi-seed without a type signal.

AdjectiveProfile.isFullyShared(), isPartiallyShared(), isSpecific() are clean boolean helpers that let ExploreResponseBuilder produce the 'category' field without leaking classification logic across the boundary.
ComparisonResult.getSummaryCounts() provides a single-pass count for all sharing categories — handlers get an efficient summary without needing to iterate the adjectives list themselves.

SemanticFieldExplorer Javadoc is a thorough class-level comment with algorithmic steps, an example walkthrough (Seed: house → Semantic class: dwellings/places), and a computational limits section — genuinely informative, not boilerplate.

QueryExecutor interface Javadoc distinguishes findCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern with explicit behavioral contracts and cross-references — high-value documentation.
ExploreOptions Javadoc notes the intentional naming asymmetry ('Explore' vs 'Exploration') — this is an honest explanation, not an echo comment.

handleBcqlQueryPost in SketchHandlers has its own four-clause try-catch that duplicates the wrapHandler exception taxonomy — the identical exception structure copy-pasted from the outer handler is a classic AI-generated debt pattern.
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial has a structural catch(Exception e) { logger.error(...); sendError(...) } that mirrors the wrapHandler boilerplate exactly.

LogDiceCalculator.relativeFrequency(long, long) method body is: if (headwordFreq <= 0) return 0.0; return (double) collocateFreq / headwordFreq; — this utility method has no callers in the production codebase (a potential dead pass-through).
QueryResults inner class has a private constructor with the comment '// Utility class - prevent instantiation' — the comment echos what the code already clearly expresses.
ConlluConverter inner finally block has the comment '// close flushes and releases the file handle' on a try-with-resources close operation — this is self-evident from the language construct.
ExplorationHandlers.parseSeedSet Javadoc says 'Parses a comma-separated seeds parameter into a cleaned, lowercased ordered set' — accurate and useful, not a restatement.
No entry/exit logs on every function; no full object dumps to console; generic names like handleData or processItem are absent.

CollocateProfileComparator has a @Deprecated single-arg constructor CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor) that bypasses GrammarConfig and falls back to a hard-coded FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN. No callers of this constructor appear in production code — it is a shim with no active external need.
The @Deprecated constructor has no @deprecated Javadoc tag with a removal timeline or migration instruction — a developer encountering it has no guidance on when it will be removed.

GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys() actively rejects 'copulas' key and logs an explicit migration error message — this shows a completed migration that left a validator in place, which is not incomplete migration debt but rather a guard against regression.

RelationType enum contains SURFACE, DEP, ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF — the enum appears to serve dual roles: the first two (SURFACE, DEP) are relation categories, while the last four are specific relation types used as relationType in RelationConfig. This mixed taxonomy could indicate an incomplete migration from a simpler enum to a richer type system.
No TODO/FIXME referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after' were found in the reviewed sources.
No mixed-format files (e.g., old-style class + new record) or parallel API surfaces (e.g., old field name + new field name) beyond the deprecated constructor.
issues_preventing_higher_score: The @Deprecated constructor in CollocateProfileComparator is a live migration shim with no recorded removal plan.

evidence: Package layout: api/ (7 files), config/ (3 files), exploration/ (4 files), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files), model/ (9 files), query/ (4 files), utils/ (2 files), collections/ (1 file), viz/ (1 file).

Every package has a clear, predictable mandate with no mixed-concern directories: query/ = BlackLab querying, exploration/ = semantic field algorithms, model/ = domain value objects, config/ = grammar configuration loading.

api/ is cohesive: all 7 files (WordSketchApiServer, ExplorationHandlers, ExploreResponseBuilder, HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) are HTTP-layer concerns. No domain logic escapes into api/.

collections/ and viz/ are single-file packages. LongIntHashMap is a specialized data structure that justifies isolation (it is a concrete memory-optimization artifact with its own contract). RadialPlot could arguably live under utils/ or api/ but is complex enough to warrant isolation.
RelationUtils in api/ has import affinity toward config/ (it maps grammar-config IDs) — a potential placement mismatch, but its fan-in from api/ handlers is high (used by ExplorationHandlers).

model/ has 9 files: AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult, PosGroup, QueryResults, RelationEdgeType, RelationType, package-info. At 9-10 files it is approaching the boundary of manageable flat structure; however, all files are cohesive domain value objects.
No straggler root-level files — all source files are in packages under pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.
indexer/blacklab/ has only 2 files (BlackLabConllUIndexer, ConlluConverter) — the sub-package name 'blacklab' accurately signals the implementation detail.
issues_preventing_higher_score: collections/ and viz/ are single-file packages that create navigation overhead without grouping benefit. RelationUtils placement could be improved.
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two single-file packages (collections/, viz/) add navigational fragmentation without providing grouping value; RelationUtils has higher affinity to config/ than api/.

evidence: QueryExecutor.findCollocations Javadoc states 'cqlPattern must start with [' and that other formats 'throw IllegalArgumentException' — the contract is stated and matches the implementation in BlackLabQueryExecutor.
LongIntHashMap.get() Javadoc: 'Returns 0 if the key is absent' — this is an unusual zero-return contract that is documented clearly at the method level.

LongIntHashMap has a restriction that Long.MIN_VALUE cannot be stored as a key (used as EMPTY_KEY sentinel) — this is documented in the class Javadoc. No validation is performed at the put() call site, so the contract is stated but not enforced.
LogDiceCalculator.compute() returns UNCOMPUTABLE (Double.NaN) for invalid inputs — clearly documented; callers are directed to use Double.isNaN() to detect it.
ExplorationResult.empty(seed) Javadoc states '@param seed the seed word; must not be null' but the method has no null check — the contract is stated but not enforced.

RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias: Javadoc says 'The returned value is always lower-cased' but the first line is 'if (relation == null) return null;' — the contract says the return is always lower-cased but it can return null. The Javadoc does not mention the null pass-through.

handleSemanticFieldExplore calls resolvedConfig.relationType().orElseThrow() immediately after validateExploreRequest already checked relType.isEmpty() — double-guarding an already-validated field; not a contract lie but a redundant safety check.
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial is documented as 'POST /api/visual/radial', returns 'image/svg+xml' — the signature and description are consistent.

CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles: Javadoc says it returns 'ComparisonResult with graded adjective profiles'; if seedNouns is null or empty it returns ComparisonResult.empty() — this is documented in the 'All collections are non-null' contract on ComparisonResult, which is honored.
ConcordanceResult sealed interface declares getSentence(), getStartOffset(), getEndOffset(), getDocId() — both implementing records (SnippetResult, CollocateResult) honor these; no mismatch visible.

ExplorationHandlers declares three private static methods (formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates) that are never called within the class — each is a one-line delegation wrapper to ExploreResponseBuilder that has no other caller, making them dead code after the refactor

In SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern, the null/empty guard on `seed` is placed AFTER the parameter has already been used in a conditional expression: `ExplorationResult.empty(seed != null ? seed : "")` — the ternary within the return is correct but the outer check at line ~140 feels redundant since callers should pass non-null seeds

CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch passes `@Nullable BlackLabIndex index` but never null-checks it before calling `index.mainAnnotatedField()` or `index.search()`, creating an implicit assumption that the field is always non-null despite the annotation suggesting otherwise

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial wraps everything in a catch(Exception) that always returns 500, which means IllegalArgumentException from JSON parsing is classified as a server error instead of the 400 that all sibling handlers produce — this is both a logic and consistency gap

In CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits, the limit is computed as `Math.min((long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, (long) records.size())` but the variable is cast to int without overflow protection — when maxResults is large (e.g. Integer.MAX_VALUE/3+1) the long multiplication can exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE before the cast

jspecify @Nullable is applied consistently to: HttpApiUtils.requireParam return, BlackLabSnippetParser extraction methods, CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates posMap parameter, ExplorationHandlers.validateExploreRequest/resolveRelationConfig/resolveExploreParams returns

RelationConfig.buildFullPattern(String) returns @Nullable String (confirmed by Javadoc 'Returns null if pattern is null') but lacks the @Nullable annotation on the return type — callers in SketchHandlers assign the result directly to String without null guard

CollocateQueryHelper is constructed with @Nullable BlackLabIndex but the index is dereferenced unconditionally in every method; the nullable annotation implies a null is valid but there is no defensive null-check before use

QueryResults.CollocateResult.rawXml() can be null — no @Nullable annotation — yet callers in SketchHandlers and CollocateQueryHelper already guard with `r.rawXml() != null ? r.rawXml() : ""`, showing awareness of the nullable contract without formalising it
ExplorationResult.getSeed() Javadoc says 'never null or blank' but the field is unguarded in the constructor — a caller passing null would silently violate the contract
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractHeadword returns @Nullable String, and the call site in CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery uses it safely with `headword != null ? getTotalFrequency(headword) : 0L`

ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is documented as 'Maximum nouns to expand per seed adjective' — the parameter controls nouns per collocate/adjective in the reverse-lookup pass, not nouns per seed; the field name is misleading compared to how it is actually used

CollocateProfileComparator is named as if it only compares, but it both fetches adjective profiles from the corpus (heavy I/O) and computes commonality/distinctiveness — the 'Comparator' suffix understates its responsibility

handleBcqlQueryPost in SketchHandlers has an HTTP-method suffix ('Post') that no other handler carries — handleSketchRequest, handleConcordanceExamples, handleVisualRadial do not encode their HTTP method in their names
ExploreOptions.topCollocates in the record is used as both the top-N collocates for the seed lookup AND the max for nouns_per in multi-seed; the dual role is not reflected in the name
The package 'collections' contains only LongIntHashMap while 'utils' has CqlUtils and LogDiceCalculator — the split creates an asymmetric utility package structure without clear rationale

buildBaseExploreResponse in ExplorationHandlers is a well-named private builder method; similarly buildRelationResponse and buildBcqlResponse in SketchHandlers follow a consistent `build*Response` convention
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer establishes a clear contract: IAE→400, IOException→500, Exception→500 — this is the intended baseline for all handlers

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial wraps its entire body in catch(Exception)→500, preventing wrapHandler's IAE→400 path from ever firing for this endpoint; a malformed JSON body returns 500 instead of 400, unlike all sibling POST handlers

ExplorationHandlers has its own inner try-catch blocks for IOException and IAE inside each handler method (e.g. handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti) — these produce correct 400/500 responses but create a double-handling layer since wrapHandler already provides the same mapping

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations wraps IllegalArgumentException as IOException: `throw new IOException("Invalid CQL pattern...", e)` — this reclassifies a client-side error (bad pattern) as an I/O error; wrapHandler will then return 500 instead of 400 for this class of input problem

CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch catches InvalidQuery (a library parse exception) and rethrows as IOException — this is consistent with the IOException-based I/O boundary contract of the QueryExecutor interface
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks lets IOExceptions propagate naturally; Main.handleBlackLabIndexCommand catches Exception at the top level — consistent for a CLI boundary
GrammarConfigLoader.load throws IOException for config parse errors — consistent with file-I/O framing

WordSketchApiServer constructor creates SemanticFieldExplorer, SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, and VisualizationHandlers but deliberately defers port binding (TCP) to start() — the separation is documented in a comment and is a correct design decision

BlackLabQueryExecutor opens the BlackLab index file in its constructor — I/O at construction time; this means test code that wants an executor needs either a real index or must mock at the interface level (QueryExecutor), not at the class level

SemanticFieldExplorer constructor calls deriveNounCqlConstraint(grammarConfig) at construction time — this performs a stream filter over grammarConfig.getRelations() but not I/O; the computation is pure and deterministic

The @Deprecated single-arg constructors in SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator create parallel init paths with fallback constants — if a test uses the deprecated constructor it gets a different pattern than production, creating an inconsistency that is acknowledged in Javadoc but still a latent risk
GrammarConfig is a loaded-at-startup immutable object passed through constructors via DI rather than fetched lazily or from a global static — this is good design, no stale-snapshot risk

No environment variables are read at class-load time; the CORS origin property is read at response-send time via getCorsAllowOrigin(), and the grammar.config property is read in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand before the server is constructed — no import-time side effects

Package 'collections' contains exactly one class (LongIntHashMap) while semantically-similar utility classes (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator) live in 'utils' — the package split creates asymmetric organization without documented rationale

Model layer has a mixed strategy: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult are traditional classes (private fields, getX() accessors), while AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, and ExplorationResult.SummaryCounts are Java records — this is documented in model/package-info.java with an explanation, so the divergence is intentional

VisualizationHandlers uses a broad inner catch(Exception)→500 convention while all other handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) rely on wrapHandler for error classification — a clear style island in error-handling convention

ExplorationHandlers declares three private static methods (formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates) that are effectively dead — no other sibling handler class has this pattern of unused private delegations

Handler class visibility is uniformly package-private (no `public` modifier) and consistently instantiated by WordSketchApiServer — this convention is correctly maintained across all four handler classes
The `ExploreResponseBuilder` is `final` while handler classes are not — a minor but inconsistent application of the `final` modifier

21 test files found covering most key subsystems: CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, GrammarConfigLoader, RelationsValidation, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateProfileComparator, SemanticFieldExplorer, ConlluConverter, RelationUtils, HttpApiUtils, HandlersTest, RadialPlot, BCQLParser

HandlersTest tests HTTP handlers using a custom MockExchange — covers missing-parameter 400 cases and some validation paths; does not test successful execution paths which would require a real QueryExecutor
MultiSeedExplorer has no dedicated test file — its behaviour is only tested through SemanticFieldExplorer integration tests that use a stub executor
ExploreResponseBuilder has no test file — the JSON serialisation of ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult/Edge is untested in isolation
WordSketchApiServer routing layer (registerRoutes, wrapHandler IAE→400 mapping) has no test — the classification contract advertised in Javadoc is not verified

BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, DependencySketchTest, HybridAlgorithmTest likely require a real on-disk index to run (integration tests) — there is no clear test tier separation and no indication of which tests are unit vs integration
SemanticFieldExplorerTest and CollocateProfileComparatorTest use stub QueryExecutors correctly — the exploration algorithm is well-tested in isolation from the index
ConlluConverterTest was recently added and tests the error path (documented in the task instructions as a new addition)

QueryExecutor interface maintains consistent parameter ordering (lemma, pattern/deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) across findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, and executeDependencyPatternWithPos

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern uses ExploreOptions to bundle 4 parameters, but exploreMultiSeed takes minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared as separate args — the same 3 parameters are bundled in ExploreOptions for single-seed but loose for multi-seed, creating an inconsistent calling convention between the two exploration paths

HTTP API mixes `relation` as a URL path segment (`/api/sketch/{lemma}/{relation}`) with `relation` as a query parameter (`/api/semantic-field/explore?relation=adj_predicate`) — both usages exist without documentation of why one pattern was chosen per endpoint
handleConcordanceExamples uses `word1` and `word2` as parameter names while handleSemanticFieldExamples uses `adjective` and `noun` — both endpoints take a word pair but parameterise it differently

The public SemanticFieldExplorer API has two overloads of exploreByPattern (a convenience form taking RelationConfig and a raw form taking individual strings) — the raw form is public but should be private since it exposes internal details (simplePattern) to callers
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates places the optional `posMap` parameter last — consistent with the recommendation to put optional parameters last in the method signature
All HTTP endpoints are public with no authentication or authorization layer — no route has an auth decorator, middleware, or permission check of any kind

CORS is the only access control mechanism: getCorsAllowOrigin() applies a uniform origin restriction to every endpoint response via setCorsHeader(exchange), called in sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, and sendOptionsResponse
The CORS origin is configurable at runtime via -Dcors.allow.origin JVM property, which is read at response-send time rather than at class-load — this is consistent and avoids stale configuration
No user sessions, no API keys, no JWT, no role-based permission strings — authorization is uniformly absent, not mixed

The server binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() (127.0.0.1) by default — a network-level access control that limits exposure to localhost; this is consistent across all endpoints since they share the same server instance

POST endpoints (/api/bcql, /api/visual/radial) accept arbitrary user-supplied patterns; BCQL has a length limit (MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024) and complexity limit (MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20) as implicit input validation, but there is no analogous limit on the radial plot items array (capped at 40 items by client-side truncation)

CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery is divided into three well-named phases (collectHits → scoreHits → rank/limit) with a record type (HitRecord) to carry inter-phase data — a coherent data-pipeline design that separates concerns within the method

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern also uses a four-phase structure (fetchSeedCollocates → buildCollocateToNounsMap → filterCandidates → identifyCoreCollocates) but the method body is ~90 lines with all four phases inlined — the phase separation is expressed through comments rather than delegation to named helpers

ExplorationHandlers has three private static methods (formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates) that are dead after the ExploreResponseBuilder refactor — the refactor improved design coherence by centralising response assembly but left dead code behind

SketchHandlers.handleBcqlQueryPost has its own 4-level exception handling (outer try→catch IOException, inner catch JSONException, catch IAE, catch Exception) inside a handler that is already wrapped by wrapHandler — this is an over-engineered exception handling structure for a single method

The SemanticFieldExplorer facade is described as 'thin' but still contains 5 private phase-helper methods and two exploration algorithms (exploreByPattern and its delegating overload) — the facade description is inaccurate; it is a medium-weight service class

ExploreResponseBuilder is a well-designed static service: all response-assembly logic is centralised, the class has no state, and the format*/build* naming distinguishes transformation from construction — a clear single responsibility

The duplicate private helpers in ExplorationHandlers (buildExploreResponseBody is a one-line delegate; formatSeed/Noun/Core methods are unreachable) indicate a partial refactor that did not fully consolidate into ExploreResponseBuilder

strengths: Strict one-direction dependency DAG: api → exploration → query → model with no cycles.
QueryExecutor interface provides a clean boundary isolating BlackLab from the exploration and api layers.
LongIntHashMap correctly quarantined in collections/ with no upward leakage.
Package-private visibility on MultiSeedExplorer and ExplorationHandlers/ExploreResponseBuilder prevents accidental cross-layer access.
Each package has a focused, intelligible mandate: no hub package that everything imports.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are shallow and isolated: one misplaced domain helper (RelationUtils) and one construction-time coupling that could be relaxed, rather than any cycle or hub problem.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase scores well here: the layering is clean, dependencies flow inward, and the one interface boundary (QueryExecutor) is used consistently. The only real concerns are RelationUtils living in api/ when it maps config-domain concepts, and direct instantiation of SemanticFieldExplorer in the server constructor rather than via the QueryExecutor contract. These are minor; there are no cycles, no shared-mutable state across boundaries, and no hub modules. This places the codebase solidly in the 80-85 range for this dimension.

strengths: Package taxonomy maps onto domain capabilities with no historical-accident orphans.
The model/ package has a clear stated contract (package-info.java) and consistent value-object usage.
MultiSeedExplorer extraction demonstrates active refactoring toward single-responsibility packages.
ExploreOptions record provides a clean, named parameter bundle — a new engineer can understand the single-seed configuration surface at a glance.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are structural rather than local: ExplorationResult serves double duty across two algorithms with different semantics, and SemanticFieldExplorer accumulates four distinct algorithms without clear sub-ownership.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The architecture is comprehensible and intentional: packages map to domain functions, model objects are clean, and recent refactors show active improvement. However, ExplorationResult's semantic overloading for multi-seed (comma-joined seed string, DiscoveredNoun semantics flipped to represent seeds rather than discovered nouns) means a new engineer reading the model will be confused about its true contract. SemanticFieldExplorer as a four-algorithm facade reduces the clarity of ownership. These are real but contained problems that push the score below the 80-85 solid range to just under it.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface adds genuine value as a BlackLab isolation boundary and testability seam.
ExploreOptions record cleanly replaces a 4-argument tuple in the single-seed path.
GrammarConfigLoader is a pure loader with no stateful surface — callers hold the value object, not the loader.
ValidatedExploreRequest DTO cleanly captures validated state and eliminates an awkward null-return parse pattern.
RelationConfig.buildFullPattern() and substituteAtPosition() hide pattern string manipulation from callers.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The main abstraction issue is a small set of no-op pass-through methods in ExplorationHandlers, rather than a systemic over-engineering or under-abstraction pattern.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Most abstractions in this codebase pay for themselves with real leverage: the interface isolates an expensive external library, the record reduces a 4-arg tuple, the DTO eliminates a multi-output pattern. The one clear defect — three zero-value private statics delegating to ExploreResponseBuilder — is a low-cost fix. The wide parameter bag on exploreMultiSeed is a minor ergonomic issue with a reasonable documented rationale. The codebase sits solidly around 80: above 'significant drag' but with room to sharpen the abstraction around multi-seed parameter bundling.

strengths: Single JSON library (fastjson2) used consistently across all serialization points — no competing parsers.
Logging uses the standard SLF4J + Logback pairing with no framework duplication.
All declared dependencies have a clear distinct purpose; no library is pulled in for light or duplicated use.
Test dependencies are properly scoped and use a consistent JUnit 5 setup.
dependency_health.issue_character: Only one low-severity concern: reliance on com.sun.net.httpserver as an implicit JDK internal rather than a declared public API dependency.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Dependency health is a clear strength of this codebase. The production dependency set is minimal, non-duplicated, and purpose-driven. The only concern is use of com.sun.net.httpserver which is a JDK internal API (not a formally supported public module); on Java 21+ this is stable in practice but not guaranteed across future JDK releases. This keeps the score just below the exemplary range.

strengths: wrapHandler delivers clean exception taxonomy in a small, readable method.
Guard-clause early-return pattern used consistently across all exploration handlers.
LongIntHashMap probe loop is clean, correct, and free of defensive sprawl.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles the full bracket-depth + quote-skip case with a single readable pass.
ExploreParams and ValidatedExploreRequest private records demonstrate balanced extraction: small, named, and scoped to where they are used.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Two handlers break the uniform error-handling contract by duplicating exception handling that wrapHandler already provides — the problems are localized to VisualizationHandlers and SketchHandlers.handleBcqlQueryPost rather than systemic.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The majority of this codebase has direct, intention-revealing internals: the guard-clause pattern is consistent, utility logic is clean, and extraction level is balanced. The score is held back by two specific violations of the established error-handling contract (VisualizationHandlers and handleBcqlQueryPost both re-catch exceptions that wrapHandler already handles), and a minor inefficiency in CollocateProfileComparator's multiple stream passes over the same data. These are fixable in place and do not indicate a systemic craft problem, placing the codebase around 76.

strengths: ExploreResponseBuilder cleanly owns all model-to-JSON translation, with no presentation logic escaping into handlers.
ValidatedExploreRequest DTO provides a typed hand-off between HTTP parsing and domain invocation.
CORS lifecycle is fully centralised with no per-handler divergence.
ComparisonResult.getSummaryCounts() eliminates repeated stream iterations at the API boundary.
AdjectiveProfile boolean helpers keep classification logic inside the model, out of the presentation layer.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The dominant issue is a semantic mismatch at the ExplorationResult seam for multi-seed mode — the same type carries different meanings in different contexts, requiring callers to apply implicit knowledge rather than relying on the type system.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The seam design is generally clean: data translation happens in one place, inputs are typed and minimal, and lifecycle concerns are centralised. The score is pulled down by the ExplorationResult semantic overloading in multi-seed mode (DiscoveredNoun represents seeds, 'seed' is CSV) which is a real seam hazard, and by the handleSemanticFieldComparison duplicated preamble. Neither is severe enough to push below 75, but together they keep the score from reaching the 82-85 range.

strengths: Most class-level and method-level Javadoc adds domain insight not derivable from the code alone.
SemanticFieldExplorer's algorithmic walkthrough example is a concrete teaching aid, not echo prose.
No trace of generic domain names (handleData, processItem) — method names are domain-specific throughout.
No noisy entry/exit logging or full object dumps to console.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The debt is concentrated in two places that duplicate the exception-handling boilerplate of wrapHandler (VisualizationHandlers and handleBcqlQueryPost), plus a small number of echo comments on self-evident operations.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase is largely free of AI-generated debt patterns: documentation is purposeful, names are domain-aligned, and defensive sprawl is absent at most boundaries. The two redundant exception-handling blocks are a clear debt pattern — identical structure copy-pasted from the outer handler — and the handful of echo comments add noise without value. These are fixable quickly and do not represent a pervasive problem, placing the score around 76: meaningfully above 'significant drag' but with real items to clean up.

strengths: No TODO/FIXME migrate/legacy markers found in the reviewed source files.
GrammarConfigLoader validates against the removed 'copulas' key — evidence that a previous migration completed cleanly with a regression guard.
No mixed-format or parallel API surfaces visible beyond the one deprecated constructor.
Active use of Java records for new value types (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun) is consistent — no mixed class/record for the same concept.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: Migration debt is minimal and concentrated in one deprecated constructor shim (CollocateProfileComparator), which has no production callers and no documented removal plan.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is largely clean of migration debt: one previous migration (copulas removal) completed with a guard in place, and no stale shims or mixed paradigms are visible elsewhere. The one exception — the @Deprecated CollocateProfileComparator constructor — is a real but low-severity issue: it is a dead shim with no active callers and no removal timeline. The RelationType enum dual-use is a mild design tension rather than a migration issue per se. This places the codebase solidly in the 82-86 range for this dimension.

strengths: Every package maps to a distinct domain capability: no mixed-concern directories.
api/ contains only HTTP-layer concerns with no domain logic leaking in.
indexer/blacklab/ sub-package correctly names its implementation detail.
model/ is a cohesive flat grouping of all domain value objects with a package-info.java documenting conventions.
No straggler root-level files — all code is under well-named package namespaces.
package_organization.issue_character: Issues are placement-level rather than structural: two single-file packages add minor navigational overhead, and RelationUtils belongs in config/ by domain affinity.
package_organization.score_rationale: Package organization is a strength of this codebase: the taxonomy is domain-aligned, each package is cohesive, and there are no flat-directory overloads or ambiguously named directories. The score is held just below 85 by the two single-file packages (collections/, viz/) that create slightly fragmented navigation for minimal grouping benefit, and the RelationUtils placement issue. A staged reorg moving LongIntHashMap to utils/collections/, RadialPlot to viz/ (or api/viz/), and RelationUtils to config/ would tighten the layout.

strengths: QueryExecutor method contracts are documented with parameter constraints, exception contracts, and behavioral distinctions between the four query methods.
LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel is well-documented with clear caller guidance.
LongIntHashMap zero-return and EMPTY_KEY sentinel contracts are stated at the class level.
ConcordanceResult sealed interface + implementing records have consistent contracts.
ComparisonResult.getSummaryCounts() and getFullyShared()/getPartiallyShared()/getSpecific() are consistent with each other and with AdjectiveProfile.isFullyShared() etc.
contract_coherence.issue_character: Contract issues are mostly cases where documented constraints are stated but not enforced (null checks missing for stated non-null params) and one case where the documented return type omits a null case (RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias).
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Most contracts in this codebase are stated and honored. The issues are: one undocumented null return path in RelationUtils (the 'always lower-cased' statement contradicts the null-pass-through), and two places where stated non-null contracts lack enforcement (ExplorationResult.empty seed, LongIntHashMap.put key). These are low-severity but real contract gaps. The overall contract picture is solid — no return-type lies, no mutations hidden behind getters — placing the score around 78, slightly below the 80 'solid but uneven' mark.

strengths: Phase separation in CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery (collect → score → rank) is explicit and well-labelled, making the algorithm easy to follow
Guard clauses at method entry (null/empty lemma checks in executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern) are used consistently across the query layer
Conditional chains in handleSketchRequest are clear: `dep` path vs surface path vs full sketch are dispatched without nesting
The validateExploreRequest / ValidatedExploreRequest pattern eliminates duplicated preamble logic across exploration handlers
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks has a clear documented comment explaining the deliberate behaviour on the error path in the finally block
logic_clarity.issue_character: The dominant issue pattern is dead code left by incomplete refactoring — private delegation wrappers that no longer have callers — rather than incorrect logic; one real correctness gap exists in the visualization handler's exception reclassification.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase largely does what it claims: control flow is predictable, phases are labelled, guard clauses are consistent. The dead delegation methods in ExplorationHandlers are clearly vestigial and harmless but represent logic that confuses readers. The VisualizationHandlers catch-all is a real defect that reclassifies client errors as 500. These issues are local rather than systemic, keeping the score comfortably above the 'significant drag' threshold but below 'exemplary'.

strengths: @Nullable annotations from jspecify are used coherently at API boundaries — all handler methods that may return null to signal 'error already sent' are annotated
The sealed ConcordanceResult hierarchy (SnippetResult | CollocateResult) uses Java's type system to model result variants, eliminating unsafe casts at consumer sites
WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS sentinel avoids returning null for missing POS data — callers get a typed string constant rather than a null they must guard
ExploreOptions is an immutable record — no null fields possible at construction since Java primitives are used for all numeric parameters
type_safety.issue_character: The annotation layer is mostly present but has gaps specifically around nullable return values from RelationConfig and nullable fields in model classes that are documented as non-null by Javadoc but not enforced by type or constructor guard.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase applies @Nullable in most critical API-boundary positions, which is above average for a Java project of this size. However RelationConfig.buildFullPattern returning null without annotation creates real risk of NullPointerException in SketchHandlers if a pattern is misconfigured, and the @Nullable BlackLabIndex pattern in CollocateQueryHelper signals something that cannot actually be null in production while misleading the type system. These are concrete gaps that prevent a higher score, though they are limited in scope and number.

strengths: Handler classes use a consistent verb-noun naming scheme: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison — the pattern is predictable across all 4 handlers
Model records use domain vocabulary consistently: CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, AdjectiveProfile, ExplorationResult — a new developer can map names to the linguistic domain without documentation
The build*/format* naming split in ExploreResponseBuilder is respected: build-prefix for construction, format-prefix for serialisation — the distinction is maintained across all 6 methods
validateExploreRequest / ValidatedExploreRequest are well-matched names that communicate the request/result lifecycle
naming_quality.issue_character: The naming issues are isolated cases of mismatch between a name's implied scope and actual behaviour, rather than a systemic vocabulary problem — most names in the codebase are domain-accurate and well-chosen.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase has a strong domain vocabulary (logDice, BCQL, collocate, seed, adjective profile) that is used consistently across layers. The handful of misleading names (nounsPerSeed, CollocateProfileComparator, handleBcqlQueryPost) are real but narrow defects — they create localised confusion without rippling through the whole system. This puts the codebase above the 'significant drag' threshold but below the 'mostly consistent' level of ~80.

strengths: wrapHandler provides a single documented error-classification contract for all registered HTTP endpoints — IAE as 400, IOException as 500, any other Exception as 500
QueryExecutor interface uniformly declares `throws IOException` so all callers have a single exception type to handle from the query layer
BlackLabSnippetParser methods that fail to parse return null or empty string rather than throwing, keeping parse errors local and non-fatal
error_consistency.issue_character: The errors are concentrated at two boundaries: a visualization handler that opts out of the wrapHandler classification contract, and a query-layer wrapping of IllegalArgumentException as IOException that promotes a client error to a server error.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a clearly designed error strategy (wrapHandler contract, IOException boundary in QueryExecutor) but it is unevenly applied. The VisualizationHandlers outlier breaks the 400/500 classification the project clearly intends, and the IAE-wrapped-as-IOException in findCollocations silently misclassifies a correctible client error. These are not catastrophic but they are real inconsistencies that would frustrate a developer debugging HTTP error codes. The score sits below 70 because the defects are in the API boundary — the highest-visibility layer — even though the core query layer is internally consistent.

strengths: Port binding is correctly deferred from the constructor to start() — construction and network side effects are separated
GrammarConfig is injected through constructors rather than accessed via a static singleton, eliminating global init-ordering risks
getCorsAllowOrigin() reads the system property at call time, not at class load — correct avoidance of stale snapshot from a module-level constant
No circular init dependencies observed — each class depends only on already-initialised collaborators passed in
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The initialization coupling issues are narrow: BlackLab index I/O at construction time limits testability, and the deprecated fallback constructors create a parallel initialization path that diverges from production.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This Java codebase handles initialization concerns well for the most part — GrammarConfig injection, deferred port binding, and property-read-at-use-time show deliberate design. The main gap is BlackLabQueryExecutor's index-opening I/O in its constructor, which is the most common 'initialization coupling' anti-pattern in Java; it's not hidden but it does force any integration test to have a real index. The deprecated fallback constructors are a minor documented risk. These are relatively contained issues that do not significantly harm the developer experience.

strengths: All handler classes are package-private and follow the same construction convention (injected dependencies, no public constructor) — consistent module boundary
The model/package-info.java explicitly documents the mixed record/class strategy, transforming what would be an unexplained inconsistency into a documented design decision
Naming follows consistent camelCase throughout — no snake_case or mixed conventions in Java identifiers
Build and format method naming conventions in ExploreResponseBuilder (build* vs format*) are applied without outliers
convention_outlier.issue_character: The outliers are localised: one error-handling style island in VisualizationHandlers, one package organization asymmetry (collections vs utils), and dead private methods in one class — none represent a systemic naming drift.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is largely convention-consistent. The documented mixed record/class strategy in the model package is intentional and explained. The main convention outliers (VisualizationHandlers' catch-all, the collections package, dead delegation wrappers) are each confined to a single location. The codebase sits above the 'significant drag' level because the outliers are few and findable, but short of 'mostly consistent' because the VisualizationHandlers error handling convention is a real style island that will surprise a developer reading sibling handlers.

strengths: The stub QueryExecutor pattern is used consistently in SemanticFieldExplorerTest and CollocateProfileComparatorTest, decoupling algorithm correctness tests from index availability
Core utilities (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap) all have dedicated unit tests
HandlersTest validates the validation/error path with MockExchange — tests are not purely happy-path
RelationsValidationTest verifies the grammar config structure, preventing silent misconfiguration
test_strategy.issue_character: The test coverage has a clear split: utility classes and exploration algorithms are well tested in isolation, while the HTTP layer (routing, error classification, response assembly) and the multi-seed exploration code are sparsely tested.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The codebase has a solid unit-test foundation for the core algorithms and utilities. However the absence of tests for ExploreResponseBuilder, MultiSeedExplorer, and the wrapHandler classification contract means that changes to the API response shape or error handling have no automated regression safety net. The lack of test tier separation (unit vs integration) makes the CI feedback loop unclear — a developer cannot easily run 'just the fast tests'. These gaps are real but the utility layer coverage prevents the score from dropping below 60.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface parameter ordering is uniform across all 6 query methods — a developer learning one method can predict the signature of the others
Optional `posMap` parameter is consistently placed last in CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates with a @Nullable annotation
The ExploreOptions record successfully bundles 4 parameters for the single-seed path, reducing argument lists from 7+ to 3
HTTP REST endpoints follow REST resource naming conventions (nouns, not verbs) for most routes
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The coherence issues are surface-area inconsistencies — the same parameters are bundled in some methods and scattered in others, and sibling endpoints use different parameter naming conventions for conceptually identical inputs.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The QueryExecutor interface is a well-designed consistent API. The cracks appear at the boundary between the exploration layer (single-seed vs multi-seed parameter conventions) and between HTTP endpoints (word1/word2 vs adjective/noun). These inconsistencies make the API somewhat unpredictable for a developer who learns one endpoint and expects the pattern to generalise. The score is above 70 because the core query interface is consistent and the HTTP surface is small; it falls below 80 because the parameter inconsistencies are in the most externally visible layer.

strengths: CORS origin enforcement is applied uniformly to every response via a single setCorsHeader call — no endpoint can accidentally omit it
Loopback-only binding provides consistent network-level isolation for all endpoints simultaneously
The BCQL endpoint applies consistent input complexity limits (length and bracket depth) to both POST /api/bcql and indirectly protects the index from expensive queries
authorization_consistency.issue_character: This is a local corpus-analysis tool with no user accounts — the absence of authorization is consistent and appropriate for the deployment context; the one minor inconsistency is uneven input size constraints between POST endpoints.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: Authorization consistency for a read-only local research tool is primarily about whether the access control approach is applied uniformly — and it is. All routes are intentionally unauthenticated, CORS is applied to every response, and the server binds to loopback. The slight deduction comes from the missing input size guard on the radial plot items (the BCQL endpoint has one, the radial endpoint does not) which creates an inconsistency in the defensive programming convention between sibling POST endpoints.

strengths: ExploreResponseBuilder has a single clear responsibility (model-to-JSON translation) and is entirely free of HTTP or query concerns
The executeRelationQueries method in SketchHandlers uses a BiFunction callback to vary response construction without duplicating the iteration loop — this avoids two nearly-identical for-loops
CollocateQueryHelper.HitRecord record cleanly packages the per-hit data that must pass between collectHits and scoreHits, avoiding a tangle of parallel lists
The ValidatedExploreRequest record in ExplorationHandlers bundles validated request state to eliminate scattered null checks across exploration handlers
design_coherence.issue_character: The design issues are concentrated around two incomplete refactors: (1) the ExploreResponseBuilder centralisation that left dead delegates in ExplorationHandlers, and (2) the handleBcqlQueryPost exception handling that was bolted onto an already-wrapped handler.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has several coherent, well-factored designs (ExploreResponseBuilder, CollocateQueryHelper pipeline, executeRelationQueries). The coherence problems are real but localised: the dead delegation layer in ExplorationHandlers shows the refactor stopped one step short of completion, and the over-engineered exception handling in handleBcqlQueryPost creates unnecessary complexity in a method that should be simple. These issues prevent the score from reaching 80 but the overall structure is solid enough to stay above 70.

issues.summary: RelationUtils in api/ owns domain knowledge (grammar config ID mappings) that belongs in config/
issues.suggestion: Move RelationUtils to the config/ package (or create config/RelationAliases.java) so the api/ package imports config/ for alias resolution, consistent with the existing dependency direction.
evidence: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES maps short aliases (adj_predicate, subject_of) to grammar-config IDs (noun_adj_predicates, noun_verbs). This mapping is a domain contract between the public API vocabulary and the grammar config, not an HTTP concern.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult reused for multi-seed results with semantic mismatch: 'seed' becomes a CSV string and DiscoveredNoun represents seeds, not discovered nouns
issues.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated MultiSeedExplorationResult model (or a sealed ExplorationResult hierarchy with SingleSeedResult and MultiSeedResult subtypes) so the two algorithms return correctly-typed results with honest field semantics.
evidence: MultiSeedExplorer.explore() returns new ExplorationResult(String.join(",", seeds), ..., discoveredNounsList, ...) where each DiscoveredNoun is actually a seed word, not a discovered noun.
ExplorationResult.getSeed() Javadoc says 'the seed word this result was built from; never null or blank' — but for multi-seed results it contains e.g. 'theory,model,hypothesis'.
ExplorationResult.getDiscoveredNouns() Javadoc says 'discovered nouns sorted by relevance score descending' — but in multi-seed mode the list contains the input seeds themselves.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer accumulates four distinct algorithms (single-seed, multi-seed, comparison, examples) without sub-ownership
issues.suggestion: Consider making SemanticFieldExplorer a true facade that accepts its two collaborators (MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) via constructor injection, and document that it is a facade over these algorithms. This makes the ownership explicit without needing to split the public API.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer exposes exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, compareCollocateProfiles, and fetchExamples as top-level public methods — four conceptually distinct operations.

exploreMultiSeed delegates to MultiSeedExplorer (a separate class), compareCollocateProfiles delegates to CollocateProfileComparator, but both delegate targets are not publicly accessible (MultiSeedExplorer is package-private), making SemanticFieldExplorer the only visible entry point.
The Javadoc for SemanticFieldExplorer describes two modes (single-seed and multi-seed) plus comparison mode — effectively three separate feature areas in one class.

issues.summary: Three private static methods in ExplorationHandlers are pure pass-throughs to ExploreResponseBuilder with no added behavior
issues.suggestion: Delete these three private statics. All callers use buildExploreResponseBody (which calls ExploreResponseBuilder.populateExploreResponse directly), so these dead delegates can be removed without impact.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.formatSeedCollocates(result) body: return ExploreResponseBuilder.formatSeedCollocates(result);
ExplorationHandlers.formatDiscoveredNouns(result) body: return ExploreResponseBuilder.formatDiscoveredNouns(result);
ExplorationHandlers.formatCoreCollocates(result) body: return ExploreResponseBuilder.formatCoreCollocates(result);
None of the three methods are called — buildExploreResponseBody delegates to ExploreResponseBuilder.populateExploreResponse() directly.

issues.summary: com.sun.net.httpserver (JDK internal API) used as the HTTP server without an explicit module declaration or alternative dependency
issues.suggestion: For a production server consider replacing com.sun.net.httpserver with a declared lightweight dependency such as the JDK's com.sun.net.httpserver via --add-modules jdk.httpserver in module-info.java, or migrate to a small stable alternative (e.g., Javalin, Undertow, or Vert.x HTTP lite). At minimum, add a module-info.java requires jdk.httpserver declaration so the dependency is explicit.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer imports com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer and uses it for all route registration.
com.sun.net.httpserver is a JDK internal package not guaranteed stable across Java versions, though it has been available since Java 6 and is present in Java 21.

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial wraps its body in catch(Exception) despite being registered via wrapHandler, producing redundant error handling
issues.suggestion: Remove the try-catch from handleVisualRadial and let exceptions propagate to wrapHandler. If IllegalArgumentException can be thrown during JSON parsing, it will correctly be converted to a 400 by wrapHandler.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer registers the handler: registerPostHandler(server, "/api/visual/radial", wrapHandler(visualizationHandlers::handleVisualRadial, "Radial plot")).
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial body: try { ... } catch (Exception e) { logger.error("Error rendering radial", e); HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 500, ...) }.
The inner catch intercepts all exceptions before wrapHandler sees them, bypassing the outer exception taxonomy (IAE→400, IOException→500) and sending 500 for all errors including client errors.

issues.summary: SketchHandlers.handleBcqlQueryPost duplicates the wrapHandler exception taxonomy in its own try-catch
issues.suggestion: Remove the try-catch from handleBcqlQueryPost; let the existing wrapHandler boundary handle exception classification. If JSONException needs special treatment, add it to wrapHandler's catch taxonomy.
evidence: handleBcqlQueryPost is registered via wrapHandler and contains its own four-clause catch (IOException, JSONException, IllegalArgumentException, Exception) that re-implements the same error responses as wrapHandler.
This means every exception thrown in handleBcqlQueryPost is caught twice — once by the inner handler and once (never reached) by wrapHandler.

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator.logTopProfiles builds expensive stream chains unconditionally at DEBUG level with no isDebugEnabled guard
issues.suggestion: Wrap the method body with if (logger.isDebugEnabled()) { ... } to skip the stream computation entirely in non-debug deployments.
evidence: logTopProfiles calls profiles.stream().filter(...).limit(...).forEach(...) twice, with sort operations, unconditionally on each compareCollocateProfiles call.
The output only goes to logger.debug(...), so in production (INFO level) the stream processing runs but produces no output.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult seam carries different semantics for single-seed vs multi-seed: 'seed' is CSV, DiscoveredNoun represents seeds not discovered nouns
issues.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated MultiSeedExplorationResult or a sealed ExplorationResult hierarchy to give the type system visibility into which mode produced the result. Alternatively, document the multi-seed semantics explicitly on ExplorationResult.empty/constructor and add a boolean isSingleSeed() accessor.
evidence: MultiSeedExplorer.explore() returns new ExplorationResult(String.join(",", seeds), seedCollocScores, seedCollocFreqs, discoveredNounsList, coreCollocatesList) — discoveredNounsList contains seed words, not discovered nouns.

ExplorationHandlers for multi-seed calls response.put("seeds", new ArrayList<>(seeds)) separately from the ExplorationResult — the seeds are available from the original request, not from result.getSeed().
ExploreResponseBuilder.populateExploreResponse is called for both modes with the same field names (seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates) but the semantics of 'discovered_nouns' differ.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison duplicates the param-parsing preamble of the other exploration handlers instead of sharing the validateExploreRequest path
issues.suggestion: Extract a validateBaseParams(exchange) helper that returns a DTO with (params, seedsRaw, exploreParams) — this DTO can be used by all handlers, with validateExploreRequest extended to add the relation field on top of it.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldComparison calls parseBaseExploreRequest, requireParam(seeds), resolveExploreParams directly instead of using validateExploreRequest.

This is because handleSemanticFieldComparison does not need a RelationConfig, so validateExploreRequest (which requires one) does not fit. The asymmetry is real but leaves duplicated parsing code in two paths.

issues.summary: handleBcqlQueryPost contains a full exception-handling block that exactly duplicates the wrapHandler taxonomy — textbook AI-generated debt boilerplate
issues.suggestion: Remove the try-catch from handleBcqlQueryPost. If JSONException (from fastjson2) needs to be treated as a 400, add it to the wrapHandler catch taxonomy (or map it as a subclass of IllegalArgumentException if the library permits).
evidence: handleBcqlQueryPost: catch (IOException e) { sendError 500 }, catch (JSONException | IllegalArgumentException e) { sendError 400 }, catch (Exception e) { sendError 500 } — all three response codes mirror wrapHandler exactly.
Since the handler is registered via wrapHandler, these catches are never reached by the outer handler; the code is an unreachable shadow copy.

issues.summary: Two echo-style comments restate what the surrounding code already expresses
issues.suggestion: Remove the echo comment from QueryResults constructor. For ConlluConverter, replace the echo comment with a brief explanation of WHY a partial error-path chunk is left open (which is actually documented in the preceding comment block — the inner comment can simply be removed).
evidence: QueryResults: private QueryResults() { // Utility class - prevent instantiation } — the private constructor already prevents instantiation; the comment adds nothing.

ConlluConverter finally block: try (BufferedWriter toClose = writer) { // close flushes and releases the file handle } — the try-with-resources statement already communicates this; the comment echoes the language semantics.

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator has a @Deprecated single-arg constructor shim with no callers in production code and no removal timeline documented
issues.suggestion: Remove the deprecated constructor entirely if there are no external callers. If it must remain for library users, add a @deprecated Javadoc tag: '@deprecated Use {@link #CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor, GrammarConfig)} instead. Will be removed in v2.0.'
evidence: @Deprecated public CollocateProfileComparator(QueryExecutor executor) { this(executor, null); } — a constructor that bypasses GrammarConfig and hard-codes the fallback adjective pattern.
The @Deprecated annotation has no accompanying @deprecated Javadoc tag naming the preferred constructor or giving a removal milestone.
No production caller of the single-arg constructor is visible in the codebase; all visible call sites pass a GrammarConfig.

issues.summary: collections/ and viz/ are single-file packages that add navigational fragmentation without grouping benefit
issues.suggestion: Staged reorg: (1) Move LongIntHashMap to utils/ or a new utils/collections/ sub-package; update all import statements. (2) Move RadialPlot to api/viz/ (since it is only called from VisualizationHandlers in api/) or retain it in viz/ if future visualization classes are planned. Validate with: mvn compile after each move.
evidence: collections/ contains exactly one file: LongIntHashMap.java.
viz/ contains exactly one file: RadialPlot.java.
LongIntHashMap is used by query-layer code; placing it under utils/ or query/support/ would co-locate it with its consumers without creating a dedicated package.

issues.summary: RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias Javadoc claims 'always lower-cased' but the method returns null when input is null
issues.suggestion: Update the Javadoc to say: 'Returns null if relation is null; otherwise returns the lower-cased canonical ID.' Or add a @Nullable annotation on the return type and update the method-level Javadoc.
evidence: Javadoc: 'Resolution is case-insensitive; the returned value is always lower-cased.'
Method body first line: if (relation == null) return null;
The null case is a real return path (input can be null) but is omitted from the Javadoc's 'always lower-cased' contract statement.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.empty(seed) documents '@param seed must not be null' but performs no null check
issues.suggestion: Add Objects.requireNonNull(seed, "seed must not be null") at the start of empty(), consistent with the stated contract and with WordSketchApiServer which uses Objects.requireNonNull for grammarConfig.
evidence: Javadoc: '@param seed the seed word; must not be null'.
